---
title: "Day Two, Session Three"
subtitle: "Visualization and Statistical Modelling"
author: "Mark Andrews"
date: today
date-format: "D MMMM, YYYY"
format:
  html:
    css: styles.css
abstract: |
  This session covers data visualization and statistical modelling in Python.
  We begin with Matplotlib, the foundational plotting library, covering the pyplot interface, common plot types, and the object-oriented API for multi-panel figures.
  We then introduce Seaborn, which builds on Matplotlib to produce statistical graphics from tidy data with minimal code.
  The third topic is plotnine, a Python implementation of the grammar of graphics that closely mirrors R's ggplot2.
  The session closes with statistical modelling using statsmodels, covering linear regression, logistic regression, and linear mixed-effects models via the formula API, together with a brief survey of the broader Python statistical modelling ecosystem.
---

```{python}
#| include: false
from IPython.core.interactiveshell import InteractiveShell
InteractiveShell.ast_node_interactivity = "last_expr"
```

## Matplotlib

Matplotlib is the foundational plotting library for Python.
Most other visualization libraries build on it or interact with it.
The pyplot interface provides a collection of functions that operate on the current figure and axes, following a style similar to MATLAB.

In Jupyter — and in Quarto, which runs the same kernel — figures are captured and displayed automatically at the end of each cell.
`plt.show()` is therefore not needed and can be omitted throughout.
In a standalone Python script there is no display infrastructure, so `plt.show()` would be required there to open the figure window.
One small housekeeping point: matplotlib functions return objects, and without `plt.show()` the text representation of those objects (something like `[<matplotlib.lines.Line2D object at 0x7f...>]`) would print above the figure.
A trailing semicolon on the last call in a cell suppresses this.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 100)
plt.plot(x, np.sin(x))
plt.xlabel('x')
plt.ylabel('sin(x)')
plt.title('Sine wave');
```

Multiple calls to `plot` add lines to the same axes.
Labels passed via the `label` argument are collected into a legend with `plt.legend()`.

```{python}
plt.plot(x, np.sin(x), label='sin')
plt.plot(x, np.cos(x), label='cos', linestyle='--')
plt.legend();
```

`plt.scatter` draws a scatter plot.
The `alpha` argument controls transparency and is useful when points overlap.

```{python}
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
xr = rng.standard_normal(80)
yr = xr + rng.standard_normal(80) * 0.6
plt.scatter(xr, yr, alpha=0.5);
```

Other standard plot types follow the same call pattern.

```{python}
#| eval: true
categories = ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D']
values     = [23, 45, 56, 32]

plt.bar(categories, values)          # vertical bars
```

```{python}
#| eval: true
plt.barh(categories, values)         # horizontal bars
```


```{python}
#| eval: true
plt.hist(rng.standard_normal(500), bins=30);   # histogram
```


```{python}
#| eval: true
plt.boxplot([rng.standard_normal(100) + i for i in range(4)]);
```


For multi-panel figures, the object-oriented API is cleaner than repeated pyplot calls.
`plt.subplots` returns a figure and an array of axes objects; each axis is then configured independently.

```{python}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 4))

axes[0].plot(x, np.sin(x))
axes[0].set_title('Sine')
axes[0].set_xlabel('x')

axes[1].scatter(xr, yr, alpha=0.5)
axes[1].set_title('Scatter')

plt.tight_layout();
```

The method names on an axis object mirror the pyplot functions, with `set_` prefixes replacing standalone functions like `xlabel` and `title`.
`plt.tight_layout()` adjusts spacing so that labels and titles do not overlap.

## Seaborn

Seaborn wraps Matplotlib with functions designed to work directly with Pandas DataFrames.
Functions accept a `data` argument and column names as strings, so there is no need to extract arrays manually.
Seaborn uses the `tips` dataset, which ships with the library, throughout this section.

```{python}
import seaborn as sns
tips = sns.load_dataset('tips')
tips.head()
```

`histplot` draws a histogram, optionally overlaid with a kernel density estimate.
The `hue` argument splits the distribution by a categorical variable.

```{python}
sns.histplot(data=tips, x='total_bill', kde=True);
```

```{python}
sns.histplot(data=tips, x='total_bill', hue='time');
```

`scatterplot` maps two continuous variables to position, with optional mappings to colour, style, and size.

```{python}
sns.scatterplot(data=tips, x='total_bill', y='tip', hue='time');
```

`regplot` adds a linear regression line with a confidence band to a scatter plot.

```{python}
sns.regplot(data=tips, x='total_bill', y='tip');
```

`boxplot` and `violinplot` summarise the distribution of a continuous variable across levels of a categorical one.

```{python}
sns.boxplot(data=tips, x='day', y='total_bill', hue='time');
```

```{python}
#| eval: false
sns.violinplot(data=tips, x='day', y='total_bill')
sns.countplot(data=tips, x='day', hue='time')
sns.barplot(data=tips, x='day', y='total_bill')
```

`lmplot` combines a scatter plot and regression line and supports faceting by a third variable into separate panels.

```{python}
sns.lmplot(data=tips, x='total_bill', y='tip', col='time');
```

## plotnine

plotnine is a Python implementation of the grammar of graphics, closely modelling R's ggplot2.
A plot is built by adding layers with `+`: an initial `ggplot` call defines the data and aesthetic mappings, geometric layers specify how variables are drawn, and further components control scales, labels, and themes.

```{python}
from plotnine import ggplot, aes, geom_point, geom_smooth
from plotnine import geom_histogram, geom_boxplot, geom_bar
from plotnine import facet_wrap, facet_grid, labs, theme_minimal
```

```{python}
(ggplot(tips, aes(x='total_bill', y='tip', color='time'))
 + geom_point(alpha=0.6))
```

`geom_smooth` adds a fitted line.
The `method='lm'` argument requests a linear fit with a confidence band.

```{python}
(ggplot(tips, aes(x='total_bill', y='tip'))
 + geom_point(alpha=0.4)
 + geom_smooth(method='lm'))
```

`facet_wrap` creates a separate panel for each level of a variable.

```{python}
(ggplot(tips, aes(x='total_bill', y='tip'))
 + geom_point(alpha=0.4)
 + facet_wrap('~day'))
```

`facet_grid` arranges panels in a two-way grid defined by two variables.

```{python}
#| eval: false
(ggplot(tips, aes(x='total_bill', y='tip'))
 + geom_point(alpha=0.4)
 + facet_grid('time~day'))
```

Labels and themes are added in the same way.

```{python}
#| eval: false
(ggplot(tips, aes(x='total_bill', y='tip', color='time'))
 + geom_point(alpha=0.6)
 + labs(title='Tips vs Total Bill', x='Total Bill ($)', y='Tip ($)')
 + theme_minimal())
```

Other geometric layers follow the same pattern.

```{python}
#| eval: false
ggplot(tips, aes(x='total_bill'))              + geom_histogram(bins=20)
ggplot(tips, aes(x='day', y='total_bill'))     + geom_boxplot()
ggplot(tips, aes(x='day'))                     + geom_bar()
```

For users coming from R, plotnine's syntax is nearly identical to ggplot2, with column names passed as strings rather than bare names.

## Statistical Modelling

statsmodels provides a formula interface for statistical models that closely resembles R's model syntax.
`smf.ols` fits ordinary least squares regression; the first argument is a formula string in which `~` separates the response from the predictors.

```{python}
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

weight_df = pd.read_csv(
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mark-andrews/pysc01/master/data/weight.csv"
)
weight_df.head()
```

```{python}
results = smf.ols('weight ~ height', data=weight_df).fit()
print(results.summary())
```

The fitted object exposes estimated coefficients, standard errors, p-values, and confidence intervals as attributes.

```{python}
results.params
```

```{python}
results.conf_int()
```

`predict` returns fitted values for the original data or for new data supplied as a DataFrame.

```{python}
results.predict(pd.DataFrame({'height': [160, 170, 180]}))
```

Multiple predictors are added with `+`.
Categorical columns are expanded into indicator variables automatically.
Interactions use `:`, and `*` includes both main effects and the interaction.

```{python}
#| eval: false
smf.ols('weight ~ height + age + gender', data=weight_df).fit()
smf.ols('weight ~ height * age', data=weight_df).fit()
```

Logistic regression is fitted with `smf.logit` and follows the same interface.

```{python}
weight_df['heavy'] = (weight_df['weight'] > 90).astype(int)
results_logit = smf.logit('heavy ~ height + age + gender',
                           data=weight_df).fit()
print(results_logit.summary())
```

Linear mixed-effects models are fitted with `smf.mixedlm`.
The `groups` argument identifies the random-effects grouping variable, and the formula can include random slopes.

```{python}
sleepstudy = pd.read_csv('data/sleepstudy.csv')
sleepstudy.head()
```

```{python}
results_lme = smf.mixedlm('Reaction ~ Days', sleepstudy,
                            groups=sleepstudy['Subject']).fit()
print(results_lme.summary())
```

```{python}
#| eval: false
# random intercept and random slope for Days
smf.mixedlm('Reaction ~ Days', sleepstudy,
             groups=sleepstudy['Subject'],
             re_formula='~Days').fit()
```

## Python for Statistical Analysis

An honest answer to "should I do my statistical analysis in Python?" is: probably not, unless you have a specific reason to.
R has a deeper and more mature ecosystem for statistical modelling.
Packages like lme4, brms, mgcv, and survival cover a range of models that either have no Python equivalent or have equivalents that are less well-developed and less widely used.
If you are working in Positron or VS Code, switching between Python and R within the same project is straightforward, and data can be shared between them using the Arrow/Feather format (`pd.DataFrame.to_feather`, `arrow::read_feather` in R) with no conversion overhead.

That said, several Python packages do cover a reasonable breadth of statistical analysis.

statsmodels covers standard frequentist regression models: OLS, GLMs, mixed effects, time series (ARIMA, VAR), and survival analysis.

PyMC is the main Bayesian modelling library.
Its syntax is similar in spirit to Stan or brms: you define priors and a likelihood, and PyMC handles sampling via NUTS or other MCMC algorithms.

```{python}
#| eval: false
import pymc as pm

with pm.Model() as model:
    alpha = pm.Normal('alpha', mu=0, sigma=10)
    beta  = pm.Normal('beta',  mu=0, sigma=10)
    sigma = pm.HalfNormal('sigma', sigma=1)
    mu    = alpha + beta * x
    y_obs = pm.Normal('y_obs', mu=mu, sigma=sigma, observed=y)
    trace = pm.sample()
```

pingouin provides a collection of frequentist tests — t-tests, ANOVA, correlation, chi-square — with a clean DataFrame-based interface and effect sizes included by default.

```{python}
#| eval: false
import pingouin as pg

pg.ttest(x=group_a, y=group_b)
pg.anova(data=df, dv='score', between='group')
pg.rm_anova(data=df, dv='score', within='time', subject='id')
pg.corr(x=df['x'], y=df['y'])
```

lifelines covers survival analysis: Kaplan-Meier estimates, log-rank tests, and Cox proportional hazards regression.

scikit-learn is the standard library for machine learning rather than statistical inference: it covers cross-validation, regularised regression, classification, clustering, and preprocessing, but it does not produce p-values or confidence intervals.
